Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Aspect Aerospace secures early funding to advance swarm-deployable VLEO satellites

Aspect Aerospace, a University of South Alabama spin-off, has secured $2.4 million to develop circuit-board-sized spacecraft that could be deployed from space into very low Earth orbit in swarms to monitor the space environment.

Circuit-board-sized satellites deployable from carrier spacecraft could dramatically reduce launch costs and enable distributed sensing networks for space domain awareness.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 1, 2026

F-35 Crashes in Nevada, Pilot Ejected Safely

An F-35 went down 25 miles northeast of Indian Springs, inside the NTTR. According to the U.S. Air Force, the pilot ejected safely and was treated for minor injuries. A U.S. Air Force F-35A Lightning II fighter jet crashed during a mission over the Nevada Test & Training Range (NTTR) on Mar. 31,...

F-35 ejection systems continue to demonstrate reliability in combat aircraft despite operational losses, underscoring the critical role of pilot safety systems in high-performance fighter operations.

See The Aviationist →

Safety Apr 1, 2026

Hawaii Air Tour Operator Suspends Flights After Fatal Crash

Airborne Aviation has suspended operations indefinitely following last week's fatal helicopter crash.

Voluntary suspension by a tour operator may signal industry-wide safety reassessment and potential regulatory pressure on Hawaii's air tour market.

See AVweb →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

The 6 Airlines With The Largest Airbus A350 Fleets Today

The A350 is the peak of modern aviation efficiency and performance. Who are the largest operators of the type in the world today?

Qatar Airways' A350 dominance reflects how wide-body efficiency has become competitive necessity rather than luxury for long-haul carriers.

See Simple Flying →

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5 establishes a structured safety risk assessment framework that operators must now systematically apply to organizational processes, not just aircraft operations.

ARK Invest, backer of OpenAI, Anthropic, Tesla and SpaceX, the Ireland Strategic Investment Fund and...

ARK Invest's backing signals institutional confidence in drone delivery logistics as a scalable last-mile solution competing with traditional air-cargo networks.

Military Apr 1, 2026

Pentagon signs seven-year deal with Boeing to triple PAC-3 seeker output

The Pentagon has signed a seven-year framework agreement with Boeing to triple the production of seekers for the…...

Boeing's PAC-3 seeker tripling under a seven-year contract signals sustained U.S. defense investment in advanced air-defense systems amid geopolitical tensions.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

LATAM becomes first South American carrier to roll out Wi-Fi on long-haul routes

LATAM Airlines has completed its first long-haul flight equipped with internet connectivity. The new service was introduced on…...

LATAM's Wi-Fi rollout signals competitive pressure for regional carriers to upgrade connectivity offerings on long-haul routes to match global standards.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Philippine Airlines profits rise to $160 million in 2025 despite higher costs

Philippine Airlines (PAL) closed 2025 with a net income of $160.4 million, a 6.1% increase from the previous…...

PAL's 6.1% profit growth despite elevated operating costs signals successful revenue management and capacity optimization in a competitive Southeast Asian market.

Safety Apr 1, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document their rationale for accepting residual risk after implementing controls, creating an auditable decision trail that demonstrates compliance with regulatory expectations.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Creating near-term lunar settlements: lessons from space history

March 16, 2026, was the 100th anniversary of Robert Goddard’s first flight of a liquid fueled rocket. It reached an altitude of 41 feet. 31 years later, in 1957, Sputnik began a lonely beep as the first satellite in orbit. In 1969, 12 years after Sputnik and 43 years after Goddard’s first flight,...

Historical space milestones achieved exponential progress over decades, suggesting lunar settlement timelines require sustained institutional commitment rather than optimistic engineering projections.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

WB-57 and Secretive RAT-55 Spotted Rehearsing for NASA’s Artemis Launch

The secretive U.S. Air Force NT-43A RAT55 was spotted flying with a NASA callsign in one of the areas reserved for the launch of the Artemis II mission, opposite to the one used by the WB-57 Canberra. NASA is set to launch the Artemis II mission on Apr. 1, 2026, at 6:24 pm EDT (22:24 […]...

NASA's Artemis II launch will employ specialized atmospheric research aircraft including the rarely-seen RAT-55 to monitor upper-atmosphere conditions during the April 2026 mission.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2 fueling underway

Fueling of the SLS is underway for an April 1 launch attempt of Artemis 2, the first mission to send humans toward the moon in more than 50 years.

Artemis 2's fueling milestone signals NASA's readiness to resume lunar human spaceflight after five decades, with implications for commercial space partnerships and aerospace supply chain sustainability.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Apr 1, 2026

Boeing's Higher MTOW 787 Dreamliners Cleared By FAA

While we’ve already covered some of this news through our March 9th video, it was only on March 23rd that the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) certified Boeing’s increased maximum takeoff weights (iMTOWs) for the 787-9 and 787-10.

FAA certification of increased 787 MTOW enables airlines to carry higher payload-to-fuel ratios, directly improving route economics and operational flexibility for long-haul operators.

See Simple Flying →

▶
General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

CID's role as a mixed-use hub demonstrates how regional airports balance general aviation training operations with commercial carrier traffic to serve broader economic development.

Leadership transitions at regional carriers like Air Astana signal potential strategic shifts in Central Asian aviation market positioning and network development.

Why space companies are betting on Ohio...

Ohio's strategic location, skilled workforce, and manufacturing infrastructure position it as a competitive hub for space supply chain and component production.

A conversation with Robert Long, President and CEO, Space Florida...

Space Florida's integrated approach to aerospace infrastructure and talent development demonstrates how regional economic policies can attract private space investment beyond traditional aerospace hubs.

Safety Apr 1, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

Reno-Tahoe's Mustang 9 Departure requires strict adherence due to terrain and airspace constraints that can create significant safety risks if procedures are not precisely followed.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Why the 787-10 uses fewer wheels than the A350-1000

Why In The World Does The Boeing 787-10 Dreamliner Have Fewer Wheels Than The Airbus A350-1000?At first glance, the landing gear of modern widebody aircraft can look almost identical. Large twin-engine jets sit low on the ground, supported by multiple wheels arranged in similar patterns, and unless...

The 787-10's lighter composite structure enables fewer landing gear wheels through advanced weight distribution engineering, reducing maintenance costs and complexity compared to the heavier A350-1000.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

Why Do Cargo Carriers Still Avoid Twin-Engine Replacements?

Older jets can still be highly valuable if they offer the right mix of range, volume, and operating economics.

Aging single-aisle freighters remain economically competitive despite twin-engine alternatives due to superior payload-range efficiency and lower acquisition costs for cargo operators.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

After three years, Artemis 2 astronauts ready to launch

Even after three years of public appearances as a crew, there can still be some surprises for the Artemis 2 astronauts.

Extended mission preparation timelines create operational risks as crew readiness extends beyond typical training cycles, challenging NASA's ability to maintain peak performance standards.

See SpaceNews →

Drones Apr 1, 2026

Finland – Authorities’ instructions for when you see a drone

The authorities and the state leadership are monitoring changes in the security situation, assessing future...

Nordic nations are establishing formal public reporting protocols for civilian drone sightings, indicating systematic national security monitoring integration into aviation awareness.

See sUAS News →

Industry Apr 1, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational efficiency improvements across all aviation layers can simultaneously address capacity constraints and environmental impact, making sustainability a competitive advantage rather than compliance burden.

See MDPI →

Airlines and airports are giving us all their annual reminder that you can't believe everything you read online...

April Fools' campaigns reveal how aviation brands leverage humor and social media timing to boost engagement while testing audience credulity in an increasingly skeptical digital environment.

The aviation world eagerly awaits the arrival of Boeing's 777X, but will it revolutionize long-haul travel as promised?

The 777X's extended range enables airlines to connect previously unserved city pairs, potentially reshaping global route networks and reducing hub dependency.

Changi Airport's Terminal 5 expansion targets pharmaceutical logistics alongside passenger growth, positioning Singapore as a critical hub for temperature-controlled cargo in Asia-Pacific.

Military Apr 1, 2026

How The Mass Production Of This Fighter Jet Keeps Its Price Down

With over a thousand F-35s built and thousands more on order, the F-35's flyaway costs can be lower than some fourth-generation fighter jets.

High-volume production economies of scale enable the F-35 to achieve lower per-unit costs than legacy fighter platforms despite greater technological complexity.

Vantor wins intelligence agency contract to monitor space objects

Under NGA’s Luno program, the company will provide ‘insights on priority objects in low Earth orbit’...

Commercial space surveillance capabilities are now critical enough for intelligence agencies to outsource monitoring of orbital assets, signaling growing reliance on private sector space infrastructure expertise.

Russian An-26 military transport crashes in Crimea, killing all 29 on board

A Russian military transport aircraft crashed in occupied Crimea on March 31, killing all 29 people on board, according to…...

The crash highlights vulnerability of aging Soviet-era transport fleets operating in conflict zones where modern air defense and maintenance standards may be compromised.
